title: How DoorDash Built an AI Shopping Assistant That Doesn’t Rely on the LLM Alone
gist: DoorDash detailed the architecture behind its conversational shopping assistant Ask DoorDash, which separates orchestration from business logic via a shared MCP tooling layer and a three-tier memory system, reporting concrete production gains (24% higher grocery checkout conversion, 17% larger baskets, 15% higher restaurant-discovery conversion) alongside an automated evaluation framework that scaled to 2,000+ daily evals and cut regression testing from six hours to twenty minutes.

themes.theme: Architecture: orchestration decoupled from business logic

insights.claim: Ask DoorDash's architecture separates an Assistant Runtime that coordinates specialized agents from a shared MCP layer that exposes business functionality (catalog search, recommendations, carts, checkout, order history, consumer memory), rather than embedding business logic directly into prompts, so multiple AI experiences can share integrations while backend capabilities evolve independently.
insights.speaker: Leela Kumili
insights.so_what: A concrete production pattern for scaling multiple AI agent experiences off shared infrastructure rather than duplicating prompt-embedded logic per feature -- relevant blueprint for enterprise AI platform teams.
insights.locator: The architecture behind Ask DoorDash separates orchestration

insights.claim: DoorDash improves latency and reliability by using deterministic, non-LLM actions to update versioned artifacts directly, and confirmation workflows for recommendations and generated carts, rather than routing every operation through the language model.
insights.speaker: Leela Kumili
insights.so_what: Counters the assumption that production AI agents should route everything through the LLM -- deterministic fallback paths are a concrete reliability/cost lever other teams can copy.
insights.locator: the platform improves latency and reliability through operational optimizations

insights.claim: Computed consumer memory improved grocery checkout conversion by approximately 24%, increased basket sizes by 17%, and reduced conversational turns by 7% during a seven-day production evaluation.
insights.speaker: Leela Kumili
insights.so_what: Concrete, quantified evidence that personalized agent memory drives measurable e-commerce conversion and basket-size gains -- a benchmark for evaluating similar AI shopping-assistant investments elsewhere.

insights.claim: The AI assistant achieved a 15% higher conversion rate than the baseline on open-ended restaurant-discovery queries.
insights.speaker: Leela Kumili
insights.so_what: Shows conversational AI discovery outperforming traditional search/browse for ambiguous intent queries -- relevant benchmark for e-commerce/marketplace AI investment cases.
insights.locator: For restaurant discovery, the assistant achieved a 15% higher conversion rate

insights.claim: Andy Fang, DoorDash co-founder, said Ask DoorDash can build a grocery cart roughly 5x faster than doing it manually, completing a cart from a single prompt in under 2 minutes.
insights.speaker: Leela Kumili
insights.so_what: A headline efficiency claim from a company insider quantifying the user-facing speed benefit of the assistant.
insights.quote: Ask DoorDash can build you a grocery cart ~5x faster than doing it manually, and that it takes a single prompt to complete your cart in under 2 minutes.

insights.claim: DoorDash's personalization layer uses three distinct memory systems -- long-term memory generated offline from historical behavior (favorite cuisines, dietary restrictions), session memory for in-conversation context, and agentic memory for explicit user-stated facts -- retrieved via semantic vector search and ranked before being injected into prompts, keeping memory management separate from model inference.
insights.speaker: Leela Kumili
insights.so_what: A reusable three-tier memory architecture pattern for any team building personalized conversational AI agents at scale.
insights.locator: DoorDash introduced an intelligence layer that manages personalization

insights.claim: DoorDash built an automated evaluation framework using LLM-generated simulated users and recorded tool fixtures that mirrors the production runtime, scaling quality measurement to over 2,000 automated evaluations daily, improving quality scores by eight points, reducing regression testing time from six hours to twenty minutes, and validating a model migration that cut latency by 35% while maintaining quality.
insights.speaker: Leela Kumili
insights.so_what: Demonstrates that heavy investment in automated agent evaluation infrastructure directly compresses release cycle time (6 hours to 20 minutes) and de-risks model migrations -- a strong ROI case study for eval tooling spend.
insights.quote: Building a useful AI agent is hard. Knowing if it is actually good is even harder.
insights.locator: Raghav Saboo, RecSys and search lead at DoorDash
